title: How to Set Up a Planted Tank: Complete Guide for Beginners
excerpt: Everything you need to set up a thriving planted aquarium. Substrate, lighting, CO2, plant selection, and step-by-step process.
content:
Low-Tech vs High-Tech: Choose Your Path

Low-tech planted tank: No CO2 injection, moderate lighting, easy plants. Lower cost, less maintenance. Perfect for beginners. Plants grow slower but still look beautiful. Good low-tech plants: Java Fern, Anubias, Java Moss, Vallisneria, Cryptocoryne, Amazon Sword.

High-tech planted tank: CO2 injection, high lighting, nutrient dosing. Faster growth, more plant species options (carpets, red plants). Higher cost and more maintenance, but stunning results. Requires understanding of the CO2/light/nutrient balance.

Choosing Substrate

Substrate is the most important decision — you can't easily change it later. For planted tanks, nutrient-rich substrates dramatically outperform inert gravel.

Best options: ADA Amazonia (gold standard, lowers pH — great for soft water species), Fluval Stratum (similar to Amazonia, more available), UNS Controsoil (excellent value), CaribSea Eco-Complete (inert but contains trace minerals). See our full substrate comparison.

Depth: 2-3 inches minimum. Slope from back (3-4 inches) to front (1.5-2 inches) for depth illusion. Use the substrate calculator to determine how much you need.

Lighting for Plants

Light is the engine of plant growth. Too little = slow growth, melting plants. Too much = algae explosion. Finding the right balance is key.

Low light (20-30 PAR): Nicrew ClassicLED, basic clip-on LEDs. For Java Fern, Anubias, Crypts. 6-8 hours per day.

Medium light (30-60 PAR): Fluval Plant 3.0, Hygger 24/7. For stem plants, most species. 7-8 hours per day.

High light (60+ PAR): Chihiros WRGB II, Twinstar. Required for carpet plants and red plants. Must use CO2 — high light without CO2 = algae farm. 6-7 hours per day.

Always use a timer. Consistency matters more than intensity.

CO2 Injection

CO2 is the #1 growth-limiting factor for aquarium plants. All the light and fertilizer in the world won't help if CO2 is insufficient.

Low-tech approach: No CO2 injection. Rely on fish respiration and surface agitation for natural CO2. Limit lighting to 6-8 hours. Choose CO2-optional plants.

Pressurized CO2: The gold standard. A cylinder, regulator, solenoid, bubble counter, and diffuser. Costs $150-300 to set up but lasts for months between refills. See our CO2 system guide.

Target: 30 ppm CO2 during lights-on period. Use a drop checker (should turn green, not blue or yellow).

Step-by-Step Setup Process

1. Hardscape first: Place rocks and driftwood before substrate for stability. Follow the aquascaping guide for design principles.

2. Add substrate: Pour and shape around hardscape. Spray with water to keep it in place.

3. Plant immediately: Don't wait — plant on day one. Remove plants from pots, rinse off rockwool, separate into portions. Use tweezers to plant stems. Attach rhizome plants (Java Fern, Anubias) to hardscape with super glue gel.

4. Fill slowly: Place a plate on substrate, pour water onto the plate to avoid disrupting plants and substrate.

5. Start filter and CO2: Run the filter, begin CO2 injection, turn on lights for only 6 hours initially.

6. Cycle the tank: Planted tanks still need cycling — use ammonia dosing method. Plants help speed the process.

7. Gradually increase light: After 2 weeks, increase to 7 hours. After a month, go to your target (7-8 hours). This reduces the initial algae bloom.

Planted Tank Maintenance

Weekly: 25-50% water change, trim overgrown stems, remove dead leaves, clean glass, dose fertilizers. Monthly: Clean filter (gently, in old tank water), check CO2 levels, replant trimmed stems. Use the maintenance schedule for a complete checklist.

title: Fish Feeding Guide: Types of Food, Schedules & How Much to Feed
excerpt: Complete fish feeding guide. Flakes vs pellets vs frozen vs live food, feeding schedules, portion sizes, and vacation feeding tips.
content:
Types of Fish Food

Flakes

The most common aquarium food. Good for surface and mid-water feeders. Breaks apart quickly — small fish love it. Downsides: creates dust, can foul water if overfed, lower quality brands use fillers. Best brands: Hikari, Omega One, Fluval Bug Bites (flake version).

Pellets (Sinking & Floating)

More nutritionally dense than flakes with less waste. Choose size based on fish mouth size. Floating pellets for surface feeders (bettas, gouramis). Sinking pellets for bottom feeders (corydoras, plecos, loaches). Best brands: Hikari Micro Pellets, Fluval Bug Bites, Northfin.

Wafers & Tablets

Designed for bottom dwellers. Algae wafers for plecos and otocinclus. Shrimp-specific wafers (Hikari Shrimp Cuisine). Bottom feeder tablets for corydoras. Drop at night for nocturnal feeders.

Frozen Food

Excellent nutrition — the best regular diet upgrade. Frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, daphnia. Thaw in a small cup of tank water, then target-feed with a turkey baster. Feed 2-3 times per week as a supplement. Don't pour the thaw water into the tank.

Live Food

The ultimate food for conditioning breeding fish and picky eaters. Live brine shrimp, daphnia, micro worms, vinegar eels, blackworms. Can culture at home. Baby brine shrimp (BBS) are essential for raising fry.

Vegetables

Blanched zucchini, cucumber, peas (deshelled), spinach, romaine lettuce. Excellent for plecos, snails, shrimp, and herbivorous fish. Blanch (boil 30 seconds, ice bath) to soften. Remove uneaten portions after 24 hours.

How Much to Feed

The golden rule: Feed only what fish consume in 2-3 minutes, twice daily. If food hits the bottom uneaten, you're overfeeding. A fish's stomach is roughly the size of its eye — it takes very little food to satisfy them.

Overfeeding is the #1 cause of water quality problems. Uneaten food decomposes into ammonia, fuels algae blooms, and clouds water. When in doubt, feed less.

Skip one day per week. In the wild, fish don't eat every day. A weekly fast improves digestion and reduces waste.

Feeding Schedule by Species Type

Community fish (tetras, rasboras, barbs): Small pinch of flakes/pellets, twice daily.

Bettas: 2-3 pellets twice daily. Fast one day per week. Prone to bloating from overfeeding.

Bottom feeders (corydoras, plecos): Sinking wafers/pellets at lights-out. 2-3 per fish.

Shrimp: Feed every other day. They graze on biofilm constantly. Shrimp-specific food + blanched vegetables.

Fry: Multiple small feedings per day (3-5x). Crushed flakes, baby brine shrimp, micro worms.

Predators (oscars, arowanas): Every 1-2 days. Larger portions of pellets, frozen food, or live food.

Vacation Feeding

1-3 days: Don't feed. Healthy adult fish handle this easily.

4-7 days: Use an automatic feeder (preset and tested before you leave). Don't use vacation feeding blocks — they foul water.

7-14 days: Automatic feeder + ask someone to do a partial water change mid-trip. Leave written instructions — non-fishkeepers always overfeed.

14+ days: Have an experienced fishkeeper visit 2-3 times per week for feeding and maintenance.

title: Aquarium Maintenance Schedule: Daily, Weekly & Monthly Checklist
excerpt: The complete aquarium maintenance checklist. What to do daily, weekly, and monthly to keep your fish healthy and tank looking great.
content:
Daily Tasks (2-3 Minutes)

Feed fish: 1-2 small feedings. Only what they eat in 2-3 minutes. See the feeding guide.

Headcount: Count your fish. A missing fish that died and is decomposing can spike ammonia rapidly. Check hiding spots.

Observe behavior: Watch for clamped fins, flashing (rubbing against objects), gasping, spots, or unusual behavior. Early detection of disease saves lives.

Check equipment: Glance at heater light, filter flow, and temperature. A stuck heater or dead filter overnight can be catastrophic.

Weekly Tasks (30-45 Minutes)

Water change (25-50%): The single most important maintenance task. Use a gravel vacuum to simultaneously siphon out debris and old water. Treat replacement water with Seachem Prime. Match temperature within 2°F.

Test water: Ammonia, nitrite, nitrate, pH using your API Master Test Kit. Record results in a log. Ammonia and nitrite should always be 0 ppm. Nitrate should stay below 20 ppm.

Clean glass: Use a magnetic algae scraper or razor blade scraper. Do this before the water change so debris falls to the substrate and gets vacuumed out.

Trim plants: In planted tanks, trim overgrown stems, remove dead leaves, replant cuttings. Floating plants may need thinning to allow light through.

Bi-Weekly Tasks (15 Minutes)

Clean filter intake: Remove and rinse the filter intake sponge/strainer. Clogged intakes reduce flow and filtration efficiency.

Top off evaporation: Replace evaporated water with treated freshwater. Note: evaporation concentrates minerals — top-off only, not water change replacement.

Monthly Tasks (30 Minutes)

Clean filter media: Rinse filter sponges and bio-media in a bucket of old tank water (never tap water — chlorine kills beneficial bacteria). Replace mechanical filter pads (floss/foam). Never replace all media at once — stagger replacements to preserve your bacterial colony.

Check equipment health: Inspect heater for cracks, verify thermometer accuracy, check airline tubing connections, clean impeller if filter flow has decreased.

Deep substrate clean: Vacuum areas of substrate you might miss during weekly maintenance — behind decorations, under driftwood.

Quarterly Tasks

Replace consumables: UV sterilizer bulbs (every 6-9 months), air stones (every 3-6 months), check-valves on airline tubing.

Full equipment inspection: Check heater suction cups, tighten hose clamps, replace worn tubing, inspect electrical cords for damage.

Test tap water: Your municipal water parameters can change seasonally. Re-test tap water pH, GH, KH, and check for ammonia/chloramine.

Seasonal Considerations

Summer: Tank temperature may rise above safe levels. Consider a fan blowing across the surface, or a chiller for sensitive species. Reduce feeding slightly as metabolism increases.

Winter: Heater works harder. Verify it's sized properly for your tank with the heater calculator. Room temperature drops can cause heater failure in undersized units.

title: Saltwater Aquarium Beginner's Guide: From Zero to Reef
excerpt: Everything a beginner needs to start a saltwater or reef aquarium. Equipment, cycling, livestock selection, and common mistakes to avoid.
content:
Freshwater vs Saltwater: What's Different?

Saltwater aquariums are more complex and more expensive than freshwater — but the stunning fish, vibrant corals, and living reef ecosystems make them worth it for many hobbyists. Key differences: saltwater requires a protein skimmer, powerheads for water flow, live rock for biological filtration, and precise salinity control. Equipment cost is roughly 2-3x freshwater. Livestock is more expensive. But the results are extraordinary.

If you've never kept fish before, start with a freshwater tank first to learn the basics of the nitrogen cycle, water changes, and fish care. If you have freshwater experience and want to level up — let's go.

Essential Saltwater Equipment

Tank: Bigger is easier — 40 gallons minimum for a beginner reef. Larger water volume means more stable parameters. A 75-gallon is ideal.

Protein skimmer: The heart of saltwater filtration. Removes organic waste before it decomposes. Sized to your tank volume. Brands: Reef Octopus, Tunze, Aqua Medic.

Powerheads: Corals and marine fish need strong water flow. 10-20x tank turnover per hour. Place to create random, turbulent flow patterns.

Lighting: For fish-only tanks, any decent LED works. For corals, you need reef-quality LEDs (AI Hydra, Ecotech Radion, Kessil) — corals are photosynthetic and need specific light spectrums.

Heater: Same as freshwater — size it properly. Target 76-80°F for most species.

Salt mix: Instant Ocean (budget), Red Sea Blue Bucket (mid-range), Tropic Marin (premium). Mix with RO/DI water to 1.025 specific gravity.

RO/DI unit: Reverse osmosis removes impurities from tap water. Essential for saltwater — tap water contains phosphates and silicates that fuel nuisance algae. A 4-stage RO/DI unit costs $50-100.

Refractometer: Measures salinity precisely. Digital or optical — both work. Test weekly.

Test kits: In addition to ammonia/nitrite/nitrate, saltwater keepers test calcium, alkalinity, magnesium, and phosphate (for reef tanks).

Live Rock & Cycling

Live rock is the foundation of a saltwater tank. It's porous rock colonized by beneficial bacteria, coralline algae, and microfauna. It serves as biological filtration, aquascaping, and homes for fish. Use 1-1.5 lbs per gallon.

Cycling a saltwater tank takes 4-8 weeks. Add live rock, dose ammonia (or use a raw shrimp), and wait for the cycle to complete — same principle as freshwater cycling but often slower.

Choosing Your First Saltwater Fish

Best beginner saltwater fish: Clownfish (iconic, hardy, captive-bred readily available), Royal Gramma (peaceful, stunning purple-yellow), Firefish Goby (beautiful, shy, easy), Yellow Watchman Goby (personality plus), Banggai Cardinalfish (elegant, easy).

Avoid as first fish: Tangs (need large tanks), Mandarin Dragonets (need mature tanks with pods), Anthias (need frequent feeding), Moorish Idol (almost impossible to keep). See our full species database for saltwater species — filter by "saltwater" and "beginner" care level.

When to Add Corals

Wait at least 3-6 months before adding corals. Your tank needs to mature — beneficial bacteria populations must stabilize, coralline algae should be growing, and you should be comfortable maintaining stable water parameters.

Beginner corals: Start with soft corals and LPS: Zoanthids (colorful, hardy), Mushroom corals (nearly indestructible), Kenya Tree (grows fast), Hammer coral (mesmerizing movement), Torch coral (similar to hammer). SPS corals (Acropora, Montipora) are expert-level — save them for later.

Common Beginner Mistakes

Adding fish too soon (cycle fully first). Adding too many fish at once (1-2 at a time, weeks apart). Using tap water instead of RO/DI. Skipping the protein skimmer. Not enough water flow. Chasing pH with additives instead of maintaining stable alkalinity. Buying fish without researching compatibility — many marine fish are territorial. Not quarantining — marine ich (Cryptocaryon) is devastating and notoriously persistent.
